Issues.Title: Debugging Blazor Server app 'Edit and Continue' says 'Applying code updates' when no source changed
Issues.Body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [x] I have searched the existing issues

### Describe the bug

When debugging a blazor server app, on hitting a breakpoint, I check a few variable values, then hit the button to continue. To be clear, zero code changes, not opening or closing any source files, just floating the mouse over some variables and then hitting the green continue arrow icon.

The 'Edit and Continue' popup appears with the message 'Applying code updates...' but I haven't changed anything.

This applying updates often takes a long time, several times longer than it takes to rebuild the entire solution from a cold restart.

Sometimes it does then continue, but often it pops up the 'Hot Reload - Unable to build changes' dialogue as per issue https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/issues/67893; which I guess is connected, but I thing seperate enough to qualify as two different issues.

Again, the error list contains lots of ENC0033 errors saying deleted attributes requires a restart, but I've not changed anything and some of the the projects that it's talking about attributes changed in haven't been altered for months.

As a result, often I am debugging by setting a breakpoint, checking variables when it hits, hitting continue, waiting for it to try to rebuild after no changes, being told it can't, stopping the application, full rebuild, moving the breakpoint to somewhere further on in the code and running again.

So moving from one breakpoint to the next can easily take 30+ seconds rather than a fraction of a second and by then I've forgotten what the variable values were, so end up having to put the breakpoint back to the first place, restart again and take screenshots of the values to compare.

This never seems to happen when I'm debugging pure code, only code and markup in razor components. If I'm working on a unit test, say, or a console app in the same solution I can move between breakpoints with zero problems and if I do need to change something am sometimes amazed at how much code I can change mid-execution and hot reload handles it without a hiccup, it only seems to be a problem when there's some razor involved. 

To be honest Blazor has always seemed a tiny bit delicate in this regard and I generally put as much code as possible into static helpers and classes rather than as 'code behind' which seems to help and is a good thing anyway I guess but there's only so much of that you can do without it getting a bit silly with almost every function in a razor page calling out to a helper or class.

I'm a big fan of Visual Studio and Blazor, used both since their inceptions; but recently it's felt like I'm spending more time fighting with Visual Studio than developing code (which isn't the case but feels that way sometimes !); it does seem like something about hot reload with Blazor drifted towards the arena of the unwell a few releases back.

### Expected Behavior

Just continuing from a breakpoint should just continue if no code changes have been made.

### Steps To Reproduce

_No response_

### Exceptions (if any)

_No response_

### .NET Version

10.0.400-preview.0.26322.102

### Anything else?

_No response_

Issues.Title: Use schema examples for XML comment examples
Issues.Body:
Fixes #65181

## Summary

Updates the OpenAPI XML comment source generator so schema-level `<example>` comments populate the OpenAPI `examples` collection instead of the singular `example` property.

This applies to XML examples on schema types and inline schema properties. Schema references already used `Examples`, so this makes generated schema output consistent with the requested OpenAPI shape.

## Changes

- Changed generated XML comment schema transformers from `schema.Example = ...` to `schema.Examples = [...]`.
- Updated source-generator assertions to validate `Examples[0]`.
- Updated generated source snapshots.
- Updated OpenAPI integration snapshots for 3.0, 3.1, and 3.2 XML documents from `example` to `examples`.

## Verification

Passed locally:

```powershell
.\.dotnet\dotnet.exe build src/OpenApi/gen/Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi.SourceGenerators.csproj --no-restore
.\.dotnet\dotnet.exe build src/OpenApi/src/Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi.csproj --no-restore /p:BuildProjectReferences=false
```

I also attempted the focused source-generator test run locally, but the broader ASP.NET Core build graph requires repo-level generated JS/native assets in this checkout, including the Blazor Web.JS output and ANCM native build artifacts.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Use schema examples for XML comment examples" (#67894)

Issues.Title: Hot Reload of Blazor Server project requires restart reporting attributes in unchanged projects prevent hot reload ENC0033.
Issues.Body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [x] I have searched the existing issues

### Describe the bug

When working on a Blazor Server application, I almost constantly have to stop and rebuild rather than being able to hot reload, even when, for example, doing something as tiny as changing a class in a div element.

[Edit] I say almost constantly; somtimes it works as expected and I can hot reload through a series of small changes, but often VS seems to get into a mood and whatever I change, however minor requires a rebuild and restart.

I get the 'Hot Reload - Changes not supported' dialogue, and on checking the Hot Reload Output pane, there are dozens of reports of ENC0033 saying that deleting an attribute requires restart, in several projects that have not been changed, as I say, even when just making a very small markup alteration to a razor component.

In the example below, I literally just fixed a text typo inside a div in a .razor file in the Hermes.Blazor project and am seeing errors for attributes being deleted in the SharedUI project, and the TestNamedPipes project, neither of which have changed, and no files of either have been opened in the UI since the last full rebuild.

For some reason my screenshot won't upload, but it's just a list of ENC0033 errors about various unchanged projects.

I appreciate this would be very hard to investigate without a copy of my solution, but it's been like this for several updates, I've rather been expecting the problem to go away and take every update when it comes available hoping to see something about hot reload in the issues resolved, but these days the release notes seem to be all about adding something called AI to everything rather than fixing bugs.

### Expected Behavior

Hot Reload to be consistently possible for small, localised changes, especially when it's just markup.

### Steps To Reproduce

Easy for me to reproduce it here, fresh start of VS, full rebuild of blazor server app, run it, change some markup in a .razor file and hit the hot reload button.

Possibly tricky elsewhere without my specific project files. I can provide evidence / diagnostics if required, no problem to make the errors happen.

### Exceptions (if any)

Sometimes it's fine, which isn't not very helpful I'm afraid, but restarting VS alone isn't a fix-all.

### .NET Version

10.0.400-preview.0.26322.102

Issues.Title: Startup Project reset on restarting Visual Studio
Issues.Body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [x] I have searched the existing issues

### Describe the bug

I have a solution with a dozen or so projects, one of which is a shared DLL type project, the rest being console applications and web applications.

Whichever project I have set as the (single) startup project, when I close and re-open Visual Studio, the startup project was always set to the shared project, which ironically enough is the only one that I would never want to be the startup project.

On looking into it, I discovered that if in doubt, VS will assign the first project in the .sln file as the startup project, so I moved the project I work on most often to the top, so that is now the one that is always the startup project on VS load.

The thing is, that's only the case maybe a quarter of the time, I work on different projects of course, and set the startup to the one I'm working on and testing, but this is never remembered over a restart.

It looks like there is, or used to be a 'DefaultStartup="true"' property that can be set in the .sln, but when I change the startup project in the UI, save it, rebuild and save again just for good measure, there's never any 'DefaultStartup' property in the .sln file.

The article that mentions this property is from 2019 so it may well not be the case any more, but I can't find anything definitive, and in any case, I set it in the UI so would expect it to be saved in whatever way it saves it.

Interestingly with a much smaller solution, I set the startup, restart it and the change is retained. This is a slightly older solution, but both .sln files are headed 'Microsoft Visual Studio Solution File, Format Version 12.00'.

I open the .slnx for a more recent solution created a few months ago and the slnx format is different, there's no header or file version, and that solution also remembers startup project changes.

So while the new one is an slnx and the two older ones are sln's, one of the sln's remembers the startup project fine, so I'm a bit puzzled as to why the other doesn't.

### Expected Behavior

Changing the startup project and restarting VS should retain the selected project.

### Steps To Reproduce

Change the startup project and restart.

### Exceptions (if any)

Works fine with some solutions, but not with the main one I work on daily.

### .NET Version

VS Insiders [12009.208]
.Net 10

### Anything else?

_No response_

Issues.Title: [Razor] State pollution in `BeginWriteTagHelperAttribute` due to uncleared `_attributeInfo` in `RazorPageBase.EndWriteAttribute`
Issues.Body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [x] I have searched the existing issues

### Describe the bug

When an HTML dynamic attribute (e.g., `dir="@Model.Direction"`) is immediately followed by a TagHelper attribute that contains the `@@` escape sequence (e.g., `<link href="~/css/@@aiursoft/app.css">`), the internal `_attributeInfo` state in `RazorPageBase` is not properly cleared. 

This causes a "dirty read" during the execution of the TagHelper attribute. Because the C# Razor compiler emits a `WriteAttributeValue` call inside `BeginWriteTagHelperAttribute()` when it splits the string at `@@`, it mistakenly inherits the stale `_attributeInfo` state and leaks the prefix of the previous HTML attribute (e.g., ` dir="`) directly into the middle of the TagHelper's generated output buffer.

### Expected Behavior

The previous attribute's state should be fully cleared when `EndWriteAttribute()` is called, preventing its prefix from polluting any subsequent TagHelper attributes. The TagHelper should correctly resolve the URL without injecting garbage text.

Expected Output:

```html
<link href="/css/@aiursoft/app.css" rel="stylesheet" />
```

### Steps To Reproduce

1. Create a standard ASP.NET Core MVC or Razor Pages project.
2. Add the following markup to any `.cshtml` file (e.g., `_Layout.cshtml`). Notice that the `<html>` tag has a dynamic attribute, followed immediately by a TagHelper attribute (`~/`) that contains an escaped `@` (`@@`):
```html
<html dir="@("ltr")">
<head>
    <link href="~/css/@@test/app.css" rel="stylesheet" />
</head>
</html>
```
3. Run the application and inspect the page source.
4. The output will be silently corrupted with the `dir="` prefix leaking into the `href` attribute:
```html
<html dir="ltr">
<head>
    <link href="/css/ dir="test/app.css" rel="stylesheet" />
</head>
</html>
```

### Exceptions (if any)

No exceptions are thrown. This is a silent HTML generation corruption bug.

### .NET Version

10.0 (Also reproducible in earlier .NET Core versions)

### Anything else?

**Root cause analysis:**
This occurs because `BeginWriteTagHelperAttribute` sets up a `StringWriter` buffer, and when the Razor Chunk Generator encounters the `@@` escape sequence, it breaks the string and emits a `WriteAttributeValue` call inside this scope. 

Because `RazorPageBase.EndWriteAttribute` failed to reset the `_attributeInfo` struct, `WriteAttributeValue` sees `_attributeInfo.AttributeValuesCount == 1` and blindly prepends the stale prefix to the new buffer.

**Proposed Fix:**
A PR has already been prepared and submitted to fix this by adding `_attributeInfo = default;` to the end of `RazorPageBase.EndWriteAttribute()`. This guarantees the attribute scope state is properly cleared.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "[Razor] State pollution in `BeginWriteTagHelperAttribute` due to uncleared `_attributeInfo` in `RazorPageBase.EndWriteAttribute`" (#67891)

Issues.Title: Fix context leak in `RazorPageBase` causing attribute prefix pollution in TagHelpers
Issues.Body:
Reset _attributeInfo to default after writing suffix. Fix #67891

Fix context leak in RazorPageBase causing attribute prefix pollution in TagHelpers

<!-- Thank you for submitting a pull request to our repo. -->

<!-- If this is your first PR in the ASP.NET Core repo, please run through the checklist
below to ensure a smooth review and merge process for your PR. -->

- [x] You've read the [Contributor Guide](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) and [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/CODE-OF-CONDUCT.md).
- [x] You've included unit or integration tests for your change, where applicable.
- [x] You've included inline docs for your change, where applicable.
- [x] There's an open issue for the PR that you are making. If you'd like to propose a new feature or change, please open an issue to discuss the change or find an existing issue.

<!-- Once all that is done, you're ready to go. Open the PR with the content below. -->

### Description

This PR fixes an edge-case bug in the Razor runtime where the internal `_attributeInfo` state is not cleared after writing an HTML attribute, leading to a "dirty read" (state pollution) when evaluating a subsequent TagHelper attribute.

### Steps to Reproduce & Symptoms

The bug manifests under a very specific combination of conditions:
1. An HTML tag with a dynamic attribute (e.g., `lang="@Model.Language"`).
2. Immediately followed by a tag that triggers a `TagHelper` (e.g., `<link href="~/[path]">`).
3. The `TagHelper` attribute contains an escaped `@` symbol (`@@`), forcing the Razor compiler to split the attribute and emit `WriteAttributeValue("", ...)` calls inside a `BeginWriteTagHelperAttribute()` block.

Because `EndWriteAttribute()` (from step 1) never clears the `_attributeInfo` state, `_attributeInfo.AttributeValuesCount` remains `1`. When `WriteAttributeValue` is subsequently called inside `BeginWriteTagHelperAttribute`, it mistakenly assumes it is still writing the previous attribute and prepends the **stale prefix** (e.g., ` lang="`) to the buffered TagHelper attribute.

This results in bizarre HTML output where the previous attribute's prefix is injected into the middle of the TagHelper attribute, corrupting the URL.

### Fix
Added `_attributeInfo = default;` to the end of `RazorPageBase.EndWriteAttribute()` to ensure that the attribute state is properly cleared when the writing scope ends. This prevents any subsequent `WriteAttributeValue` calls in buffered scopes from picking up stale prefixes.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Fix context leak in `RazorPageBase` causing attribute prefix pollution in TagHelpers" (#67890)

Issues.Title: ForwardedHeadersMiddleware has no options hot-reload, and ForwardedHeadersOptions fails to compile with the Configuration Binding source generator (CS0102/CS0111 on IPNetwork)
Issues.Body:
## Summary

While implementing runtime (restart-free) reconfiguration of forwarded-headers trust
(e.g. adding a new custom/vanity host to `AllowedHosts` when a new tenant is added in a
multi-tenant app), I hit two related problems with `ForwardedHeaders`:

1. `ForwardedHeadersMiddleware` snapshots its options at construction and never observes
   `IOptionsMonitor` changes, so options can't be hot-reloaded without an app restart.
2. `ForwardedHeadersOptions` cannot be bound via the **Configuration Binding source
   generator** — enabling it produces a **build error** — which blocks the natural,
   options-based way of working around problem

### Environment
- .NET 10 / ASP.NET Core 10.0
- `Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration.Binder` source generator (`<EnableConfigurationBindingGenerator>true</EnableConfigurationBindingGenerator>`)

---

## Problem 1 — No hot-reload of `ForwardedHeadersOptions`

`ForwardedHeadersMiddleware` reads `IOptions<ForwardedHeadersOptions>` **once in its
constructor** and precomputes internal state (`AllowedHosts`, `KnownNetworks`,
`KnownProxies`). Because the middleware is a singleton, changing the options at runtime via
`IOptionsMonitor<ForwardedHeadersOptions>.OnChange` (backed by a reloading configuration
source such as `appsettings.json` `reloadOnChange`, Azure App Configuration, etc.) has no
effect until the process is restarted.

This is inconsistent with other first-party middleware that already support live reload:
- `HostFilteringMiddleware` recomputes its allowed-hosts state on options change
  (via `IOptionsMonitor` / `IOptionsChangeTokenSource`).
- `HttpLoggingMiddleware` reads `IOptionsMonitor<HttpLoggingOptions>.CurrentValue` per request.

### Ask
Make `ForwardedHeadersMiddleware` consume `IOptionsMonitor<ForwardedHeadersOptions>` and
recompute its cached state on change (mirroring `HostFilteringMiddleware`), so
`UseForwardedHeaders()` honors configuration reloads without a restart.

---

## Problem 2 — `ForwardedHeadersOptions` breaks the Configuration Binding source generator

With `<EnableConfigurationBindingGenerator>true</EnableConfigurationBindingGenerator>`, binding
`ForwardedHeadersOptions` from configuration fails to compile. The generator emits **duplicate**
helper members for the `IPNetwork` type used by `ForwardedHeadersOptions.KnownNetworks`.

### Repro
```csharp
// Boilerplate.Server.Shared.csproj
<EnableConfigurationBindingGenerator>true</EnableConfigurationBindingGenerator>

// any startup code
var options = new ForwardedHeadersOptions();
configuration.GetSection("ForwardedHeaders").Bind(options);
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "ForwardedHeadersMiddleware has no options hot-reload, and ForwardedHeadersOptions fails to compile with the Configuration Binding source generator (CS0102/CS0111 on IPNetwork)" (#67886)

Issues.Title: API Proposal: Add OnPageChanging and OnPageChanged event support to Paginator
Issues.Body:
## Background and Motivation

The QuickGrid `Paginator` component in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.QuickGrid` ships with a fully styled UI for navigating between pages, but it does not expose any way for consumers to be notified when a page change occurs. This forces developers who need to integrate QuickGrid with server-side or service-based data sources to:

- Re-implement the Paginator UI from scratch, or
- Subclass `Paginator` and override its internal navigation flow.

Both workarounds duplicate logic the framework already owns (button rendering, URL-based navigation, query-string state syncing) and are not supported surface area, so they are fragile across updates.

A small, additive pair of event callbacks on `Paginator` would let consumers observe the pagination lifecycle, request new data on page change, and react to the post-change state without subclassing or duplicating the component.

This proposal adds:

- `OnPageChanging` — invoked before `PaginationState.SetCurrentPageIndexAsync` is called, while `CurrentPageIndex` still reflects the previous page.
- `OnPageChanged` — invoked after `PaginationState.SetCurrentPageIndexAsync` completes, with `CurrentPageIndex` already updated.

Both callbacks receive the target/new page as a 1-based, user-facing number.

## Proposed API

```diff
namespace Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.QuickGrid;

public partial class Paginator : IDisposable
{
     [Parameter, EditorRequired] public PaginationState State { get; set; } = default!;

     [Parameter] public RenderFragment? SummaryTemplate { get; set; }

+    /// <summary>
+    /// A callback that is invoked immediately before the page changes.
+    /// The callback receives the target page number as a 1-based, user-facing value
+    /// (for example, the first page is <c>1</c>, not <c>0</c>).
+    /// This is invoked before <see cref="PaginationState.SetCurrentPageIndexAsync(int)"/> is called,
+    /// so <see cref="PaginationState.CurrentPageIndex"/> still reflects the previous page when the callback runs.
+    /// </summary>
+    [Parameter] public EventCallback<int> OnPageChanging { get; set; }
+
+    /// <summary>
+    /// A callback that is invoked immediately after the page has changed.
+    /// The callback receives the new page number as a 1-based, user-facing value
+    /// (for example, the first page is <c>1</c>, not <c>0</c>).
+    /// This is invoked after <see cref="PaginationState.SetCurrentPageIndexAsync(int)"/> has completed,
+    /// so <see cref="PaginationState.CurrentPageIndex"/> reflects the new page when the callback runs.
+    /// </summary>
+    [Parameter] public EventCallback<int> OnPageChanged { get; set; }
}
```

The corresponding `PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt` entries:

```diff
+ Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.QuickGrid.Paginator.OnPageChanged.get -> Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.EventCallback<int>
+ Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.QuickGrid.Paginator.OnPageChanged.set -> void
+ Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.QuickGrid.Paginator.OnPageChanging.get -> Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.EventCallback<int>
+ Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.QuickGrid.Paginator.OnPageChanging.set -> void
```

Internally, a new helper centralizes the lifecycle so both the initial query-string sync and `NavigationManager.LocationChanged` go through the same path:

```csharp
internal async Task HandlePageChangeAsync(int newPageIndex)
{
    await OnPageChanging.InvokeAsync(newPageIndex + 1);
    await State.SetCurrentPageIndexAsync(newPageIndex);
    await OnPageChanged.InvokeAsync(newPageIndex + 1);
}
```

## Usage Examples

### Observing the previous vs. new page in component

```razor
<Paginator State="@pagination"
            OnPageChanging="RememberPreviousPage"
            OnPageChanged="LogNewPage" />

@code {
    private void RememberPreviousPage(int upcomingPage) {  }
    private void LogNewPage(int newPage) { }
}
```

## Alternative Designs

- **Subclass and override `Paginator`.** Considered and rejected: the existing `Paginator` does not expose any overridable page-change method, and the navigation logic in `OnParametersSetAsync` / `OnLocationChanged` is not extension-friendly. Forcing consumers to subclass or copy the entire `Paginator.razor` markup to add a single hook is disproportionate.
- **Expose a single combined event with a state enum (`Changing` / `Changed`).** Rejected: less discoverable, breaks Blazor's idiomatic two-callback pattern (`OnClick` / `OnChange`, `OnInput` / `OnValueChanged`), and forces consumers to branch on the enum.
- **Add the callbacks to `PaginationState` instead of `Paginator`.** Considered: `PaginationState` is the source of truth for the page index, but consumers expect the event to be raised when the user clicks the Paginator's buttons (UI interaction), not whenever any code updates the state. Keeping the events on `Paginator` scopes them to the component that drives the change.
- **Use `EventCallback<PaginationState>`.** Rejected: a `PaginationState` reference leaks implementation details and makes it harder to document the simple 1-based-page contract. `EventCallback<int>` matches the user-facing semantics described in the XML doc.
- **Similar APIs in other ecosystems.** React component libraries (e.g., MUI `TablePagination`, Ant Design `Pagination`) expose `onChange(page, pageSize)` and `onShowSizeChange` callbacks. Splitting the callback into pre- and post-change variants is more Blazor-idiomatic and matches `OnChange`/`OnValueChanged` patterns used elsewhere in Blazor (e.g., `EditContext.OnFieldChanged`).

## Risks

- **Behavior change for query-string initial sync.** Previously, when the URL's page parameter differed from `State.CurrentPageIndex` on the first render, `Paginator` called `State.SetCurrentPageIndexAsync` silently. After the change, that call goes through `HandlePageChangeAsync`, so user-supplied `OnPageChanging`/`OnPageChanged` handlers run during `OnParametersSetAsync`. This is the intended behavior, but consumers who relied on the silent sync may observe a new callback invocation on the first render. The fix in this PR documents this explicitly in the `OnPageChanging`/`OnPageChanged` XML doc comments.
- **Exception propagation from `OnPageChanging`.** If `OnPageChanging` throws, `State.SetCurrentPageIndexAsync` is not called and `OnPageChanged` is not invoked. This is intentional (it lets consumers cancel or short-circuit a page change) but differs from "fire and forget" expectations. The behavior is covered by tests in `PaginatorEventTest.cs`.
- **No new public surface on `PaginationState`.** `PaginationState` is unchanged, so the proposal is purely additive on `Paginator` and does not affect existing consumers.
- **Performance.** The added work per page change is two `EventCallback` invocations on already-allocated delegate instances. No measurable rendering or allocation regression is expected; no hot path is altered.
- **Public API surface growth.** Two new `[Parameter]` properties are added; the change is captured in `PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt` so the API baseline reflects it before the next release ships.

Issues.Title: Expose Authorization Result and Add Forbidden State to Blazor AuthorizeView
Issues.Body:
## Summary `AuthorizeViewCore` currently calls `IAuthorizationService.AuthorizeAsync(...)` to determine whether a user is authorized, but only keeps the boolean `Succeeded` value from `AuthorizationResult`. The complete authorization result, including `AuthorizationFailureReason` values, is discarded before rendering. This prevents applications from displaying meaningful authorization failure messages in Blazor UI. This proposal updates `AuthorizeViewCore` to preserve the full `AuthorizationResult` and expose authorization failure details through authorization-related template contexts. The design adds: 1. A new `AuthorizationStateWithResult` type that extends `AuthenticationState` and includes `AuthorizationResult`. 2. A new `Forbidden` render fragment for authenticated users who fail authorization. 3. Updated fallback behavior so `NotAuthorized` can receive an `AuthorizationStateWithResult` instance when authorization fails and `Forbidden` is not supplied. This addresses both linked issues: - - --- ## Problem Blazor's `AuthorizeViewCore` currently evaluates authorization and renders one of the existing fragments: - `Authorizing` - `Authorized` - `ChildContent` - `NotAuthorized` The authorization result is reduced to a boolean value: ```csharp result.Succeeded ``` As a result, metadata from `AuthorizationResult` is lost. This is a problem because custom authorization handlers can produce useful failure reasons: ```csharp context.Fail( new AuthorizationFailureReason( this, "Missing permission: Project.Read")); ``` Today, `AuthorizeView` does not provide a supported way for Razor markup to display that failure reason. This creates two related gaps. ### Gap 1: Authorization Result Is Not Exposed Applications cannot access the following from `AuthorizeView` UI templates: ```csharp AuthorizationResult AuthorizationFailure AuthorizationFailureReason ``` This prevents applications from showing detailed authorization failure messages. ### Gap 2: Authenticated Forbidden Users Are Not Separated from Anonymous Users Today, both anonymous users and authenticated unauthorized users generally flow through `NotAuthorized`. Applications often need different UI: ```text Anonymous user: Please sign in. Authenticated but unauthorized user: You are signed in, but you do not have permission. ``` A dedicated `Forbidden` state gives applications a clearer way to represent the authenticated-but-not-authorized case. --- ## Goals / Non-Goals ### Goals 1. Preserve the full `AuthorizationResult` returned by `IAuthorizationService.AuthorizeAsync`. 2. Expose authorization failure reasons to Blazor templates. 3. Add a dedicated `Forbidden` render fragment for authenticated-but-not-authorized users. 4. Preserve existing `NotAuthorized` source compatibility. 5. Allow `NotAuthorized` fallback scenarios to access authorization result information without changing the public `NotAuthorized` property type. 6. Preserve existing behavior when applications do not use the new API. 7. Keep the design simple for common Razor usage. 8. Avoid changing policy evaluation, role evaluation, resource authorization, or authentication behavior. 9. Provide a path that satisfies both scenarios: exposing the authorization result and separating forbidden UI from anonymous/not-authorized UI. ### Non-Goals 1. Do not change authorization policy semantics. 2. Do not change authentication behavior. 3. Do not remove or rename existing `AuthorizeView` parameters. 4. Do not require applications to use `Forbidden`. 5. Do not automatically sanitize, localize, or rewrite authorization failure messages. 6. Do not introduce a general-purpose diagnostics API for authorization. 7. Do not change routing authorization behavior outside the scope of `AuthorizeViewCore`. --- ## Detailed Design ### Overview The proposed design changes `AuthorizeViewCore` so that the component stores the full `AuthorizationResult` instead of only storing whether authorization succeeded. The design introduces a new context type: ```csharp AuthorizationStateWithResult ``` This type derives from `AuthenticationState` and adds an `AuthorizationResult` property. The same type is used by: 1. `Forbidden` 2. `NotAuthorized` fallback scenarios where authorization failed This design fixes both issues: - `Forbidden` provides a clear, explicit 403-style rendering path for authenticated users who fail authorization. - `NotAuthorized` can still access authorization failure details when `Forbidden` is not supplied. --- ### New Context Type Add a new public type: ```csharp namespace Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Authorization; public sealed class AuthorizationStateWithResult : AuthenticationState { public AuthorizationStateWithResult( ClaimsPrincipal user, AuthorizationResult authorizationResult) : base(user) { AuthorizationResult = authorizationResult; } public AuthorizationResult AuthorizationResult { get; } } ``` ### Purpose `AuthorizationStateWithResult` represents the current authentication state plus the result of authorization evaluation. It allows existing `AuthenticationState`-based templates to continue working while providing access to authorization metadata in failed authorization scenarios. --- ### Authorization State Storage `AuthorizeViewCore` should preserve the full authorization result internally. Conceptually, instead of storing only whether authorization succeeded, the component stores: ```csharp private AuthorizationResult? currentAuthorizationResult; ``` The `null` state represents that authorization is still in progress. When no authorization data applies to the component, the result is treated as successful: ```csharp currentAuthorizationResult = AuthorizationResult.Success(); ``` When authorization data is present, the component stores the full result returned by the authorization service: ```csharp currentAuthorizationResult = await AuthorizationService.AuthorizeAsync( user, Resource, policy); ``` --- ### Existing Fragments The existing fragments remain: ```csharp RenderFragment? ChildContent RenderFragment? Authorized RenderFragment? NotAuthorized RenderFragment? Authorizing ``` ### New Fragment Add: ```csharp RenderFragment? Forbidden ``` --- ### Rendering Behavior The conceptual rendering behavior is: ```csharp if (authorizationResult is null) { render Authorizing; } else if (authorizationResult.Succeeded) { render Authorized or ChildContent with AuthenticationState; } else { var stateWithResult = new AuthorizationStateWithResult( currentAuthenticationState.User, authorizationResult); if (currentAuthenticationState.User.Identity?.IsAuthenticated == true && Forbidden is not null) { render Forbidden with stateWithResult; } else { render NotAuthorized with stateWithResult; } } ``` This means: - `Forbidden` is preferred for authenticated failed authorization. - `NotAuthorized` remains the fallback path. - Both failed authorization paths can access the full `AuthorizationResult`. --- ### Rendering Matrix | Scenario | Rendered Fragment | Context Type | |---|---|---| | Authorization is in progress | `Authorizing` | No context | | Authorization succeeds | `Authorized` or `ChildContent` | `AuthenticationState` | | User is anonymous and authorization fails | `NotAuthorized` | `AuthorizationStateWithResult` | | User is authenticated and authorization fails, `Forbidden` is supplied | `Forbidden` | `AuthorizationStateWithResult` | | User is authenticated and authorization fails, `Forbidden` is not supplied | `NotAuthorized` | `AuthorizationStateWithResult` | | No authorization data applies | `Authorized` or `ChildContent` | `AuthenticationState` | --- ## Public API Surface ### Ref Assembly Format ```csharp namespace Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Authorization { public sealed class AuthorizationStateWithResult : Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Authorization.AuthenticationState { public AuthorizationStateWithResult( System.Security.Claims.ClaimsPrincipal user, Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization.AuthorizationResult authorizationResult); public Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization.AuthorizationResult AuthorizationResult { get; } } public abstract partial class AuthorizeViewCore : Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.ComponentBase { [Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.ParameterAttribute] public Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.RenderFragment< Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Authorization.AuthorizationStateWithResult>? Forbidden { get; set; } } } ``` --- ## `NotAuthorized` Render Fragment Behavior The `NotAuthorized` public API remains unchanged: ```csharp [Parameter] public RenderFragment? NotAuthorized { get; set; } ``` However, when authorization fails, the runtime context passed to `NotAuthorized` is an instance of: ```csharp AuthorizationStateWithResult ``` Because `AuthorizationStateWithResult` derives from `AuthenticationState`, this remains source-compatible with existing `NotAuthorized` usage. Existing code continues to work: ```razor

Please sign in.

 ``` Advanced code can access the authorization result when needed: ```razor @{ var authorizationState = state as AuthorizationStateWithResult; var reason = authorizationState?.AuthorizationResult.Failure? .FailureReasons.FirstOrDefault()?.Message; }

@(reason ?? "You are not authorized.")

 ``` This behavior directly addresses the request to expose `AuthorizationResult` through the not-authorized rendering path while avoiding a breaking change to the existing `NotAuthorized` property type. --- ## `Forbidden` Render Fragment Behavior `Forbidden` is rendered when: 1. Authorization has completed. 2. Authorization failed. 3. The current user is authenticated. 4. The application supplied a `Forbidden` fragment. Example: ```razor

Please sign in.

@(state.AuthorizationResult.Failure?.FailureReasons.FirstOrDefault()?.Message ?? "You do not have permission to view this project.")

``` `Forbidden` provides the preferred, user-friendly API for the authenticated-but-not-authorized case. --- ## Customer Scenario / Use Cases ### Use Case 1: Permission-Based Authorization A project management application requires `Project.Read`. When a signed-in user does not have the required permission, the custom authorization handler returns: ```text Missing permission: Project.Read ``` The application can show this message in `Forbidden`: ```razor

@(state.AuthorizationResult.Failure?.FailureReasons.FirstOrDefault()?.Message ?? "You do not have permission.")

``` --- ### Use Case 2: License-Based Feature Restrictions A SaaS application restricts features by license. When a user is authenticated but not licensed for a feature, the authorization handler can return a failure reason such as: ```text Your license does not include Advanced Reporting. ``` The UI can display this in `Forbidden`, allowing the application to explain why a feature is disabled or unavailable. --- ### Use Case 3: Existing Apps Using `NotAuthorized` An existing app may only have: ```razor

Access denied.

 ``` This app continues to work. If the app wants access to the authorization result without adding `Forbidden`, it can cast the runtime context: ```razor @{ var stateWithResult = state as AuthorizationStateWithResult; } @if (stateWithResult is not null) {

@(stateWithResult.AuthorizationResult.Failure? .FailureReasons.FirstOrDefault()?.Message ?? "Access denied.")

} else {

Access denied.

} ``` This fixes the scenario where a user wants the result of `AuthorizeAsync` available from the not-authorized UI path. --- ### Use Case 4: Separate Anonymous and Forbidden UI Applications can separate anonymous and forbidden cases: ```razor

Please sign in to manage billing.

You are signed in, but you do not have permission to manage billing.

``` This provides clearer UI and better matches the 401/403 distinction. --- ## Proposed API Usage Examples ### Example 1: Basic Forbidden UI ```razor

Please sign in to view reports.

You are signed in, but you do not have permission to view reports.

 ``` --- ### Example 2: Show First Authorization Failure Reason ```razor

@(state.AuthorizationResult.Failure?.FailureReasons.FirstOrDefault()?.Message ?? "You do not have permission to view this project.")

Please sign in.

 ``` --- ### Example 3: Use `NotAuthorized` Fallback with Authorization Result ```razor @{ var authorizationState = state as AuthorizationStateWithResult; var reason = authorizationState?.AuthorizationResult.Failure? .FailureReasons.FirstOrDefault()?.Message; }

@(reason ?? "You are not authorized.")

 ``` --- ### Example 4: Display Multiple Failure Reasons ```razor @if (state.AuthorizationResult.Failure?.FailureReasons.Any() == true) {
    @foreach (var reason in state.AuthorizationResult.Failure.FailureReasons) {
  • @reason.Message
    • }
} else {

You do not have permission to access this resource.

}

Please sign in.

 ``` --- ### Example 5: Custom Authorization Requirement ```csharp public sealed record ProjectPermissionRequirement(string Permission) : IAuthorizationRequirement; ``` --- ### Example 6: Custom Authorization Handler ```csharp public sealed class ProjectPermissionHandler : AuthorizationHandler { protected override Task HandleRequirementAsync( AuthorizationHandlerContext context, ProjectPermissionRequirement requirement) { if (context.User.HasClaim("Permission", requirement.Permission)) { context.Succeed(requirement); } else { context.Fail(new AuthorizationFailureReason( this, $"Missing permission: {requirement.Permission}")); } return Task.CompletedTask; } } ``` --- ### Example 7: Policy Registration ```csharp builder.Services.AddAuthorizationCore(options => { options.AddPolicy("ProjectRead", policy => { policy.RequireAuthenticatedUser(); policy.Requirements.Add(new ProjectPermissionRequirement("Project.Read")); }); }); builder.Services.AddScoped(); ``` --- ## Alternatives Considered ### Alternative 1: Change `NotAuthorized` Type to Include `AuthorizationResult` Example: ```csharp public RenderFragment? NotAuthorized { get; set; } ``` #### Rejected This would be a breaking change because existing users provide `RenderFragment`. The proposal avoids this by keeping the public property type unchanged: ```csharp RenderFragment? ``` and passing a derived runtime type only when authorization fails. --- ### Alternative 2: Only Add `Forbidden` and Do Not Update `NotAuthorized` #### Rejected This solves the authenticated forbidden scenario but does not fully address the ask from `#43959`, where developers want authorization result information available from the unauthorized rendering path. It also leaves fallback `NotAuthorized` without failure details when `Forbidden` is not supplied. --- ### Alternative 3: Add a Separate `NotAuthorizedWithResult` Fragment Example: ```csharp public RenderFragment? NotAuthorizedWithResult { get; set; } ``` #### Rejected This adds another template and creates overlapping concepts: - `NotAuthorized` - `NotAuthorizedWithResult` - `Forbidden` That would make the component harder to understand. --- ### Alternative 4: Add Public `AuthorizationResult` Property Example: ```csharp public AuthorizationResult? AuthorizationResult { get; } ``` #### Rejected The result is needed inside render fragments. Passing the result as template context is more natural for Razor authoring. --- ### Alternative 5: Use Cascading Value #### Rejected A cascading value would require additional component structure and make the common scenario more complex. --- ## Compatibility Analysis ### Source Compatibility Existing code continues to compile: ```razor

Access denied.

 ``` No existing render-fragment property is removed or renamed. --- ### Binary Compatibility The proposal is additive. It adds: ```csharp AuthorizationStateWithResult AuthorizeViewCore.Forbidden ``` Existing public members remain unchanged. --- ### Behavioral Compatibility Existing apps that do not use `Forbidden` continue to render `NotAuthorized` when authorization fails. The main behavioral enhancement is that the runtime context passed to `NotAuthorized` in failed authorization cases may be an `AuthorizationStateWithResult`, which is still an `AuthenticationState`. Existing code that only uses: ```csharp state.User ``` continues to work. --- ### Razor Compatibility Existing Razor templates remain valid: ```razor @state.User.Identity?.Name ``` Advanced templates can opt into the richer runtime type: ```razor @{ var stateWithResult = state as AuthorizationStateWithResult; } ``` --- ## Risks and Limitations ### Risk 1: Runtime Type Is Richer Than Compile-Time Type for `NotAuthorized` `NotAuthorized` remains typed as: ```csharp AuthenticationState ``` but in failed authorization cases receives: ```csharp AuthorizationStateWithResult ``` This is compatible but less discoverable than `Forbidden`. #### Mitigation Document that `Forbidden` is the recommended template for accessing authorization failure details. Document that `NotAuthorized` fallback can cast to `AuthorizationStateWithResult` when needed. --- ### Risk 2: Sensitive Failure Messages Failure reasons may include internal permission names or policy details. #### Mitigation Documentation should warn developers to display only user-safe failure messages. --- ### Risk 3: Additional API Surface The proposal adds one type and one render fragment. #### Mitigation The new API is small and focused. --- ### Risk 4: Multiple Failure Reasons Authorization can fail for multiple reasons. #### Mitigation Documentation and tests should show how to enumerate reasons. --- ### Risk 5: Failed Authorization Without Failure Reasons Some failures may not include `AuthorizationFailureReason`. #### Mitigation Samples should always include fallback UI text. --- ## Testing Plan ### Unit Tests Add tests for: 1. `Authorizing` renders while authorization is pending. 2. `Authorized` renders when authorization succeeds. 3. `ChildContent` renders when authorization succeeds and `Authorized` is not supplied. 4. `Forbidden` renders for authenticated users when authorization fails. 5. `NotAuthorized` renders for anonymous users when authorization fails. 6. `NotAuthorized` renders as fallback when authorization fails and `Forbidden` is not supplied. 7. `Forbidden` receives `AuthorizationStateWithResult`. 8. `Forbidden` can access `AuthorizationResult`. 9. `Forbidden` can access a single `AuthorizationFailureReason`. 10. `Forbidden` can access multiple `AuthorizationFailureReason` values. 11. `NotAuthorized` fallback receives an `AuthorizationStateWithResult` when authorization fails. 12. `NotAuthorized` fallback can access `AuthorizationResult` by casting. 13. Authorization succeeds when no authorization data applies. 14. Existing validation for `ChildContent` and `Authorized` conflict remains unchanged. 15. Existing validation for unsupported authentication schemes remains unchanged. ### Recommended Test Names ```csharp RendersForbiddenContentForAuthenticatedUnauthorizedUser RendersNotAuthorizedContentForAnonymousUnauthorizedUser FallsBackToNotAuthorizedWhenForbiddenIsNotSupplied ForbiddenReceivesAuthorizationStateWithResult ForbiddenCanAccessAuthorizationFailureReason ForbiddenCanAccessMultipleAuthorizationFailureReasons NotAuthorizedFallbackReceivesAuthorizationStateWithResult NotAuthorizedFallbackCanAccessAuthorizationFailureReason RendersAuthorizedContentWhenAuthorizationSucceedsEvenWhenForbiddenIsSupplied RendersAuthorizedContentWhenAuthorizeDataIsNull ``` --- ### E2E Tests Add E2E tests using a policy that fails with a custom authorization handler. Scenario: 1. Sign in as an authenticated user. 2. User does not have required permission. 3. Policy fails with: ```text Missing permission: Project.Read ``` Verify: 1. `Forbidden` content renders when supplied. 2. Failure reason is shown in `Forbidden`. 3. `NotAuthorized` fallback can show the failure reason when `Forbidden` is not supplied. 4. Existing authentication layout still renders correctly. --- ## Documentation Updates Documentation should cover: 1. New `Forbidden` fragment. 2. Difference between `NotAuthorized` and `Forbidden`. 3. New `AuthorizationStateWithResult` type. 4. Accessing `AuthorizationResult` from `Forbidden`. 5. Accessing `AuthorizationResult` from `NotAuthorized` fallback using a cast. 6. Displaying failure reasons. 7. Handling multiple failure reasons. 8. Security guidance for displaying failure messages. Suggested wording: ```text Use Forbidden for authenticated users who fail authorization. The Forbidden context is AuthorizationStateWithResult and provides access to AuthorizationResult. NotAuthorized remains an AuthenticationState-based template. In failed authorization fallback scenarios, the runtime context may be AuthorizationStateWithResult, allowing applications to cast and access AuthorizationResult when needed. ``` --- ## Open Questions ### 1. Should the Type Name Be `AuthorizationStateWithResult`? Recommended for this design because it clearly communicates that the type is an `AuthenticationState` plus an authorization result. Alternative names: ```csharp AuthorizeViewState AuthorizeViewAuthorizationState AuthorizationViewContext ``` --- ### 2. Should `NotAuthorized` Runtime Context Include Result for Anonymous Failures? Recommended: yes. If authorization failed and `NotAuthorized` is selected, passing `AuthorizationStateWithResult` consistently allows access to the failure result. --- ### 3. Should `Forbidden` Be Preferred Over `NotAuthorized` for Failure Details? Recommended: yes. `Forbidden` is the cleaner API for authenticated failed authorization. `NotAuthorized` result access exists for compatibility and fallback scenarios. --- ### 4. Should Failure Reasons Be Sanitized? Recommended: no. The framework should expose the raw `AuthorizationResult`. Applications decide what is safe to display. --- ## Final Recommendation Proceed with this API: ```csharp public sealed class AuthorizationStateWithResult : AuthenticationState { public AuthorizationResult AuthorizationResult { get; } } ``` and: ```csharp [Parameter] public RenderFragment? Forbidden { get; set; } ``` Keep existing `NotAuthorized` API unchanged: ```csharp [Parameter] public RenderFragment? NotAuthorized { get; set; } ``` But when authorization fails and `NotAuthorized` is rendered, pass an `AuthorizationStateWithResult` instance as the runtime context. This design fully addresses both issues: - `#43959`: authorization result is preserved and available to unauthorized UI. - `#65056`: authenticated-but-not-authorized users can be handled by a dedicated `Forbidden` template with access to failure reasons. It is additive, compatible, practical for customers, and keeps the recommended user experience simple through the new `Forbidden` template.

Issues.Title: Add `AuthorizationPolicy.CombineAsync` overload that accepts endpoint metadata
Issues.Body:
introduced in https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/pull/67765.

## Background and Motivation

Only endpoint routing's `AuthorizationMiddleware` honors all three kinds of authorization metadata:
`IAuthorizeData` (e.g. `[Authorize]`), an `AuthorizationPolicy` used directly as metadata, and
`IAuthorizationRequirementData` (a custom attribute carrying requirements).

Other components that make the same authorization decision only honor a subset:
MVC `AuthorizeFilter`, SignalR `HubMethodDescriptor`, and Blazor `AuthorizeViewCore`/`AuthorizeRouteView`.
This leads to inconsistent behavior for the same attribute across subsystems (see #63365).

We need a single, shared API that takes all metadata associated with an endpoint and returns the
effective policy, so every subsystem can behave identically.

## Proposed API

```diff
namespace Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;

public class AuthorizationPolicy
{
+    public static Task<AuthorizationPolicy?> CombineAsync(IAuthorizationPolicyProvider policyProvider, IEnumerable<object> metadata);
}
```

The overload inspects each metadata item and combines any `IAuthorizeData`, `AuthorizationPolicy`,
and `IAuthorizationRequirementData` instances into one effective policy. A single item that implements
more than one of these interfaces contributes to each. Returns `null` when no authorization metadata
is present.

## Usage Examples

```csharp
// Combine every authorization-relevant metadata item on an endpoint.
var policy = await AuthorizationPolicy.CombineAsync(policyProvider, endpoint.Metadata);

if (policy is not null)
{
    // enforce policy...
}
```

## Alternative Designs

- Keep the existing `CombineAsync(provider, IEnumerable<IAuthorizeData>)` and have each subsystem
  re-implement the requirement-data/policy-instance handling. Rejected: duplicates logic and keeps
  behavior divergent.
- Extend the frozen legacy non-endpoint-routing MVC path (`EnableEndpointRouting=false` + `UseMvc`).
  Rejected: deliberately-frozen path untouched for ~7 years; not worth the risk.

## Risks

Low. The new overload is additive and the requirement-data handling is lifted from the existing
`AuthorizationMiddleware` code path, so behavior there is unchanged. Routing MVC, SignalR, and Blazor
through it changes their observable behavior to honor `AuthorizationPolicy` and
`IAuthorizationRequirementData` metadata, which could enforce policies that were previously ignored.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Add `AuthorizationPolicy.CombineAsync` overload that accepts endpoint metadata" (#67870)

Issues.Title: Design for Provide the column resize support to Quick Grid component
Issues.Body:
## Provide the column resize support to Quick Grid component

QuickGrid lacked built-in column resizing, making it difficult for users to adjust column widths based on content and layout needs. The implemented enhancement adds column resizing support, allowing column widths to be adjusted interactively and configured through the component model.

## Motivation and goals

- Provide native column resizing support in QuickGrid #47494.
- Allow users to resize columns using pointer (mouse, touch) and key down interactions.
- Enable developers to define column widths in code.
- Remove the need for CSS-based resizing workarounds discussed in the issue.
- Support keyboard-driven resizing with min/max constraints so the feature is fully usable without a pointer.

## In scope

- Add support for resizing QuickGrid columns.
- Support interactive width adjustment through the column header UI.
- Support specifying column widths through component configuration.
- Integrate resizing behavior into the existing QuickGrid experience.

## Out of scope

- Column reordering.
- Hierarchical row layouts.
- Advanced spreadsheet-style grid interactions.

## Risks / unknowns

- User-defined widths may result in very narrow or very wide columns.
- Resizing behavior depends on overall table layout and available space.
- Existing custom CSS may influence the visual resizing experience.
- The grid does not persist resized widths itself; host applications must opt in to a storage mechanism.
- Resize operations on virtualized grids only touch the currently visible rows; behavior is consistent with non-virtualized grids.

## API surface

```razor
    /// <summary>
    /// If true, the grid renders column resize handles in the header row and allows users to drag to resize columns.
    /// </summary>
    [Parameter] public bool ColumnResize { get; set; }
```

### Edge case scenarios

- Resizing a column to the minimum allowed width.
- Resizing a column containing long text values.
- Multiple columns being resized independently.
- Grids containing a mix of resized and default-width columns.
- Resizing via keyboard on a focused handle with min/max constraints in effect.

## Examples

```razor
    <QuickGrid Items="@people" ColumnResize="true">
        <PropertyColumn Property="p => p.Name" Title="Name" Width="250px" />
        <PropertyColumn Property="p => p.Email" Title="Email" />
    </QuickGrid>
```

## Details design

- A resize handle is rendered in each resizable column header; pointer/touch drag and keyboard (arrow keys ±5px, Shift+arrow ±20px) both adjust the width live until the operation ends, with min/max constraints applied throughout.
- The width is stored on the column definition and applied to header and data cells, so it is reused across re-renders without per-row recomputation.
- States: Default uses the configured or computed width; Resizing updates the width in real time; Completed applies the final width to header and data cells.
- Accessibility: handles expose an `aria-label` (e.g. "Resize column"), receive keyboard focus with a visible focus indicator, and announce width changes to assistive technologies in line with WCAG 2.1 AA.
- The grid does not persist resized widths itself; host applications opt in to storage (LocalStorage, backend store, URL params) and supply the value back through the column's `Width` on the next render.
- CSS hooks: a `col-resize-handle` class marks the handle, a `col-resizing` class is toggled on the column during drag, and the resolved width is applied as an inline style, all using existing QuickGrid class names so no new CSS files are required.
- Virtualization: only the visible rows are reflowed during drag, behavior matches non-virtualized grids from the user's perspective, and no per-row recomputation is performed for columns with a fixed width.

## Considered alternatives

- Continue relying on custom CSS-based resizing workarounds.
- Require developers to set only fixed widths without interactive resizing.
- Leave column sizing entirely to browser table-layout behavior.
- Use third-party grid solutions when column resizing is required.

Issues.Title: Design for Provide the RowDetailsTemplate support to QuickGrid component
Issues.Body:
## Provide the RowDetailsTemplate support to QuickGrid component QuickGrid did not provide a built-in way to render expandable row details, a capability requested by users and commonly available in other grid components. The implemented change adds row details support through a template-based API, allowing additional content to be rendered beneath a data row while leveraging Blazor’s component rendering model. ## Motivation and goals - Add native row details functionality to QuickGrid, addressing the feature request raised in Issue #54930, #46356. - Enable developers to render additional per-row content using a templated approach. - Support scenarios where row detail content can take advantage of Blazor rendering and asynchronous loading patterns mentioned in the issue discussion. - Provide a QuickGrid solution comparable to “row details” capabilities found in other data grid implementations referenced by the issue. ## In scope - Introduce RowDetailsTemplate support to the QuickGrid component. - Allow rendering of additional detail content associated with individual grid rows. - Render row detail content using a template that receives the current row item context. - Enable row details directly within the QuickGrid rendering pipeline rather than requiring custom grid implementations. ## Out of scope - General table enhancements such as arbitrary support for colgroup, colspan, rowspan, and broader table-layout customization. The related issue was tracked separately and only linked as a duplicate. ## Risks / unknowns - Detail templates can render arbitrary UI, so large or complex detail content may increase rendering cost. - Row detail content is user-supplied template content, making layout and styling dependent on the consuming application. - Behavior of detail content that performs asynchronous operations depends on the implementation provided by the application. - Each expanded row instantiates its own template, so memory and render cost scale with the number of simultaneously expanded rows; use judiciously on very large datasets. ## API surface ```razor /// /// Optional template for displaying expandable row details. When supplied, the grid renders a toggle button in each row, and the expanded details content appears in a separate row below the item row. /// The expanded state is tracked by , so it remains stable across data refreshes. /// [Parameter] public RenderFragment? RowDetailsTemplate { get; set; } /// /// Raised after a row's details are expanded, with the row item as its argument. /// [Parameter] public EventCallback OnRowExpanded { get; set; } /// /// Raised after a row's details are collapsed, with the row item as its argument. /// [Parameter] public EventCallback OnRowCollapsed { get; set; } ``` Added RowDetailsTemplate support to QuickGrid. The new API is template-based and provides access to the current row item when rendering details. ## Edge case scenarios - A grid row has no meaningful detail data; the template can render empty or minimal content for that item. - Detail content performs asynchronous data loading after the row is rendered. - Different rows render substantially different detail content sizes. - A detail template renders nested components with their own state and lifecycle behavior. - Multiple rows are expanded at the same time, each with its own template instance and state. ## Examples ```razor
Email: @person.Email
``` ```razor @if (personDetails is null) {
Loading…
} else {
@personDetails.Bio
} ``` ## Detailed design - Add a dedicated expand/collapse icon column for each QuickGrid row; the default indicator is a themeable caret (Unicode `▶` / `▼` or a CSS-styled SVG) and can be overridden via CSS. - Maintain row expansion state and toggle it when the icon is clicked; the toggle is also keyboard-activatable (Enter/Space) and exposes `aria-label` values that reflect the current state. - Render a details row directly below the corresponding data row when expanded. - Use RowDetailsTemplate to generate the details content for the selected item. - Expansion state is keyed by `ItemKey`, so an expanded row remains expanded across data refreshes; without `ItemKey`, tracking is position-based and is not preserved on refresh. - When expansion happens, focus moves to the first focusable element inside the rendered details, and expansion/collapse is announced to assistive technologies (WCAG 2.1 AA). - Optional programmatic control is exposed through `OnRowExpanded` and `OnRowCollapsed` event callbacks so host code can drive expansion. - Test coverage: render with and without the template, toggle via click and keyboard, state persistence across data refresh, multiple simultaneously expanded rows, async loading inside the template, and `ItemKey`-based stability. ## Considered alternatives - Continue using custom implementations outside QuickGrid instead of providing built-in row details support. - Display additional row information in separate UI surfaces (dialogs, panels, or pages) rather than inline within the grid. - Introduce a dedicated row-details component instead of a flexible template-based API. - Address row details as part of broader table feature enhancements rather than a focused QuickGrid enhancement.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Design for Provide the RowDetailsTemplate support to QuickGrid component" (#67867)

Issues.Title: Blazor WASM on IIS Express: Unable to correctly rewrite content-encoding of precompressed static file
Issues.Body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [x] I have searched the existing issues

### Describe the bug

I have racked my brains on this issue for a whole day but seem to have no success on this. Previously being on .NET 8, I was able to serve a WASM web app for local dev with precompressed Brotli files with extensions like `.wasm.br`, `.js.br`, `.data.br` (Unity webgl artifacts) without a problem using IIS Express with the following `web.config` (see below). As expected, all of the above precompressed files would be served with the expected content-type and encoding.

Now after upgrading to .NET 10, the file  `wwwroot/assets/webgl/Build.framework.js.br` returns a 200 with the correct raw data (brotli-encoded JS),  with `content-type: text/javascript` (correct) and `content-encoding: gzip` (wrong). Because of the wrong encoding returned, the browser does not automatically decode the brotli compression to show a normal JS file (which is what my application expects).

All other files with `.wasm.br` or `.data.br` seem to correctly pass through the PreCompressedBrotli condition on IIS. I sanity-checked it by changing it to something else like `value="foo"` and it reflects when fetching said resources.

Interestingly enough, if I specify a non-existent filename like `Foo.framework.js.br`, the response is (obviously) 404 with the correct `br` encoding, implying that that filename does go through the intended rewrite section.

Is there a something new on .NET 10 that changes how static files are served? 

```xml
<!-- web.config -->
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<configuration>
  <system.webServer>
    <rewrite>
      <outboundRules rewriteBeforeCache="false">
        <rule name="Set content type of compressed JS" preCondition="PreCompressedJs">
          <match serverVariable="RESPONSE_CONTENT_TYPE" pattern="^$" />
          <action type="Rewrite" value="text/javascript" />
        </rule>
        <rule name="Set content type of compressed WASM" preCondition="PreCompressedWasm">
          <match serverVariable="RESPONSE_CONTENT_TYPE" pattern="^$" />
          <action type="Rewrite" value="application/wasm" />
        </rule>
        <rule name="Set content type of compressed data" preCondition="PreCompressedData">
          <match serverVariable="RESPONSE_CONTENT_TYPE" pattern="^$" />
          <action type="Rewrite" value="application/octet-stream" />
        </rule>
        <rule name="Set content encoding of brotli files" preCondition="PreCompressedBrotli">
          <match serverVariable="RESPONSE_CONTENT_ENCODING" pattern="^$" />
          <action type="Rewrite" value="br" />
        </rule>
        
        <preConditions>
          <preCondition name="PreCompressedJs">
            <add input="{PATH_INFO}" pattern=".*\.js\.br"/>
          </preCondition>
          <preCondition name="PreCompressedWasm">
            <add input="{PATH_INFO}" pattern=".*\/assets\/webgl\/.*\/.*.wasm\.br"/>
          </preCondition>
          <preCondition name="PreCompressedData">
            <add input="{PATH_INFO}" pattern=".*\/assets\/webgl\/.*\/.*.data\.br"/>
          </preCondition>
          <preCondition name="PreCompressedBrotli">
            <add input="{PATH_INFO}" pattern=".*\.br" />
          </preCondition>
        </preConditions>
      </outboundRules>
    </rewrite>
  </system.webServer>
</configuration>

```

Issues.Title: Design for CellClass support to PropertyColumn and TemplateColumn in QuickGrid
Issues.Body:
## Add CellClass support to PropertyColumn and TemplateColumn in QuickGrid

QuickGrid did not provide a way to assign CSS classes to individual cells based on the current row item's data. Users could style content inside a TemplateColumn, but not the table cell itself, and switching from PropertyColumn to TemplateColumn could require manually handling behaviors such as sorting. The implemented fix adds cell-level CSS class support for both PropertyColumn and TemplateColumn, enabling dynamic styling based on the row context.

## Motivation and goals

- Allow QuickGrid cells to be styled based on the current row item's values #53982, #45657.
- Remove the need for CSS workarounds that attempt to style parent <td> elements indirectly.
- Preserve the usability of PropertyColumn, including its built-in behaviors, while enabling conditional cell styling.
- Provide a consistent mechanism for cell-level styling across both PropertyColumn and TemplateColumn.

## In scope

- Add support for assigning CSS classes to individual cells using row-item context.
- Enable the feature in PropertyColumn and TemplateColumn.
- Apply the generated class to the rendered table cell (td) rather than only to content inside the cell.

## Out of scope

- New row-level styling APIs.
- General theming or visual redesign of QuickGrid.

## Risks / unknowns

- Cell-class delegate logic executes per rendered cell, so inefficient implementations may increase rendering cost.
- Generated class names rely on application-provided logic and may reference CSS classes that are not defined.
- Dynamic styling behavior depends on row-item data and may change as data updates.

## API surface

```razor
    /// <summary>
    /// An optional factory that is used to compute the CSS class name for body cells in this column.
    /// </summary>
    /// <remarks>
    /// This only applies to body cells for data rows, not header cells or placeholder rows.
    /// If both <see cref="Class"/> and <see cref="CellClass"/> are provided, the resolved values are combined
    /// into a single class attribute value (with <see cref="Class"/> first).
    /// If <see cref="CellClass"/> returns <see langword="null"/>, the resolved class falls back to
    /// <see cref="Class"/>; if <see cref="Class"/> is also <see langword="null"/>, no class is applied.
    /// </remarks>
    [Parameter] public Func<TGridItem, string?>? CellClass { get; set; }
```

- Added cell-class support to PropertyColumn and TemplateColumn.
- New API allows computing a CSS class from the current row item context and applying it to the cell.

## Edge case scenarios

- A cell-class delegate returns an empty or null value; the cell renders without an additional CSS class.
- Different rows in the same column produce different CSS classes based on item data.
- PropertyColumn continues to use its existing column functionality while applying conditional cell styling.
- TemplateColumn content styling and cell styling can be used independently.

## Examples

```razor
    <QuickGrid Items="@People">
        <PropertyColumn Property="@(p => p.BirthDate)" Sortable="true" CellClass="@(p => p.BirthDate.Year < 2000 ? "table-success" : "table-warning")" />
    </QuickGrid>
```

This example applies different CSS classes to the generated table cells based on each row's BirthDate value while retaining PropertyColumn behavior such as built-in sorting.

## Considered alternatives

- **Row-level styling API (`RowClass` on the grid):** Rejected because it styles the whole `<tr>` and cannot express per-cell, data-driven classes without extra workarounds.
- **CSS-only workarounds using `:has()` or sibling selectors:** Rejected because `:has()` is not supported in older browsers/WebViews and couples data shape to CSS selectors.
- **Template-based approach (switch to `TemplateColumn` and style inner content):** Rejected because it discards `PropertyColumn`'s built-in sorting/formatting and styles the wrong element.
- **Generic attribute dictionary (`CellAttributes`):** Rejected because it is over-scoped for the common need, blurs presentation with semantics, and invites misuse.
- **`CellClass` on `TemplateColumn` only:** Rejected because the motivation is to keep `PropertyColumn` users on `PropertyColumn` while still allowing conditional cell styling.

## Breaking Changes

- None. `CellClass` is a new optional parameter that defaults to `null`, so existing grids render exactly as before.
- Existing `Class` parameter behavior is unchanged and continues to work on its own.
- No public API, signature, or rendered output is modified for consumers that do not opt in.

## Implementation Details

- The `CellClass` delegate is invoked once per body cell render, receiving the current `TGridItem`, and the returned string is merged with `Class` in the order `Class` + space + `CellClass` on the rendered `<td>`.
- If `CellClass` returns `null` or an empty string, only `Class` is used; if both are `null`, no `class` attribute is emitted.
- Header cells and placeholder rows (e.g. loading/empty states) are not affected, matching the `CellClass` XML remarks.

## Security Considerations

- Blazor renders the class attribute value through its standard HTML attribute encoding, so the value produced by `CellClass` cannot introduce HTML or script injection.
- Class names are produced from application code, so undefined classes simply have no styling effect and do not throw; developers are responsible for choosing valid CSS identifiers.
- The delegate runs in the rendering context, so it should not perform I/O, mutate state, or surface sensitive row data through the class name; keep the delegate pure and side-effect free.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Design for CellClass support to PropertyColumn and TemplateColumn in QuickGrid" (#67872)

Issues.Title: The non-scaffolded razor page "Register" does not recognise Password.RequiredLength.
Issues.Body:
### Describe the bug
Setting password length through 
```
public void ConfigureServices(IServiceCollection services){
  ...
  services.Configure<IdentityOptions>(options =>
  {
    options.Password.RequiredLength = 1;
  });
  ...
```
does not change the non-scaffolded razor page as it always says "The Password must be at least 6 and at max 100 characters long." if inputed password is < 6 characters.

### To Reproduce
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a new ASP.NET Core Web 2.1 application
2. Select "Web application (Model-View Controller)" 
3. Select "Change authentication"
3.1. Select "Individual User Account"
4. Select Ok to have the project scaffolded.
5. Open file Startup.cs
6. Add 
```
services.Configure<IdentityOptions>(options =>
{
  options.Password.RequiredLength = 1;
});
```
to method `ConfigureServices`.
7. Press F5 (or select your favourite start-debug method).
8. In the web browser that pops up
8.1. Select Register
8.2. Type "a@example.com" as user name (or select your favourite fast-to-type email addresss).
8.3. Type "a" as password (or any password shorter than 6 characters).
8.4. Select Register

### Expected behavior
The web page should have been updated with information about successful creation of user.

### Actual behaviour
The web page says "The Password must be at least 6 and at max 100 characters long."

### Additional context
I have a question at stack overflow https://stackoverflow.com/questions/53620708/how-do-i-set-password-options-in-aspnet-core-2-1 that explains the same issue if this is not clear enough.

This issue might be duplicate of #774.

Issues.Title: Remember me isPersistent understanding
Issues.Body:
This is more of a question but its not enough of a question that i could post it up on stack overflow without risking it just getting closed as primary opinion based or to broad.   So i am going to put it here in hopes of getting an answer.

The issue is the understanding of how remember me is supposed to work.    My problem is that its not doing what we would expect it to do.   

Management expects when a user logs in and clicks remember me that they will then not be required to type their login and password again for a month.  Whether or not they will still have to type in the 2fa code is up for debate.

What is actually happening is that i can close the browser reboot and come back for about an hour and 15 minutes and then i will be forced to type my password again.    This still happens even with a valid `.AspNetCore.Identity.Application` cookie set that is not due to expire for another month.

After digging around in the code i found that Remember me is actually isPersistent and it is defined as.

>Flag indicating whether the sign-in cookie should persist after the browser is closed.

Which brings you to:

There are two different types of Cookies:

- session cookies, held in memory, and which expire once the browser exits
- persistent cookies, which have a time-to-live, are persisted on disk, and are sent by the browser until their time-to-live has elapsed.

All this being said remember machine 2fa works as expected i am not prompted again for the 2fa and i have a cookie on my machine `Identity.TwoFactorRememberMe`.

So I guess my question is why are we not getting a `Identity.FactorRememberMe` or why isn't sign in respecting a long life time of the `AspNetCore.Identity.Application` cookie?

How is this supposed to work exactly?   I am following the code here even though we have normal MVC and not razor pages like you have in the example.  I am still just calling 

>var signInUserResult = await _signInManager.PasswordSignInAsync(userName, password, rememberMe, true);

Note: I am using Asp .net Identity in Identity server 4 application.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Remember me isPersistent understanding" (#2079)

Issues.Title: Login doesn't show registered social logins after error
Issues.Body:
I'm using ASPNET Core 2.1 with Razor Pages UI Library and noticed that whilst registered social logins appear when I open the Login page, they disappear after an error like incorrect password.

There seems to be a bug in Identity\Pages\Account\Login.cshtml.cs as 
````
 public IList<AuthenticationScheme> ExternalLogins { get; set; }

````
is only initialised in OnGetAsync(), but if an error happens OnPostAsync() then Page() is returned so OnGetAsyn() doesn't get called. Changing error handling in  OnPostAsync() so RedirectToPage() is returned seems to fix the problem.

Please confirm this is a bug and comment on my fix

Issues.Title: Should IdentityUser be in the EntityFramework namespace?
Issues.Body:
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.aspnetcore.identity.entityframeworkcore.identityuser?view=aspnetcore-1.1

I'm looking through various implementations from the [readme](https://github.com/aspnet/Identity/blob/master/README.md) and I've noticed they all use `IdentityUser ` for their concrete type to pass to `UserManager`, or have had to craft a custom type to be able to have a contract between the database and the .NET AspNetIdentity implentation they've written (see the DynamoDB library).

I've had to do this too for the [Marten implementation I've written](https://github.com/yetanotherchris/Marten.AspNetIdentity), and the reason is, a `TUser` that your `UserStore` takes, should really be an interface - it will always have an email, most likely an Id of some sort, firstname, lastname and then a bag of properties.

Things become even more abstract when dealing with claims.

If everyone is using `IdentityUser` or their own custom `TUser` doesn't this defeat the purpose of AspnetIdentity - which is to be able to switch and change implementations easily? That isn't possible if your consumer is tightly coupled with a `TUser` implementation of the library.

### Example `TUser` implementations

- [MongoIdentityUser](https://github.com/tugberkugurlu/AspNetCore.Identity.MongoDB/blob/master/src/AspNetCore.Identity.MongoDB/MongoIdentityUser.cs)
- [DynamoIdentityUser](https://github.com/miltador/AspNetCore.Identity.DynamoDB/blob/master/src/AspNetCore.Identity.DynamoDB/DynamoIdentityUser.cs)
- [Redis uses `IdentityUser<string>`](https://github.com/aguacongas/Identity.Redis/blob/develop/src/Aguacongas.Identity.Redis/UserStore.cs)
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Should IdentityUser be in the EntityFramework namespace?" (#2056)

Issues.Title: [Question] Passwords should not be of type String ?
Issues.Body:
My security team escalated to me that the `PasswordHasher` is vulnerable to heap inspection, as the password is of type `String`. 
It should be instead a byte array / span of bytes cleared at the end of the process, as it is done by any cryptographic operation in corefx. CC @bartonjs.
Same problem with any password related types (`IPasswordValidator`, and maybe also the `IUserPasswordStore`)

Issues.Title: Feedback: ensure that .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling only supported in VS 2017 and won't be supported in VS 2019
Issues.Body:
Apologize if my feedback may sound controversial or may spark a debate.

I need to be sure that the problem mentioned on this [page](https://github.com/aspnet/Tooling/blob/master/known-issues-vs2017.md#to-create-aspnet-core-10--11-projects-install-visual-studios-net-core-10---11-developer-tools-for-web-component) about .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling is installed when creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 project should not happen or should not be supported in VS 2019.

These are my main concerns:

1. VS 2019 is about to be released in around early 2019
2. ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 is not going to be supported anymore, based on [this page](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/microsoft-support.md).
3. Creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 will always install the .NET Core 1.0/1.1 web tooling, and this is not necessary when previous reason has reached the unsupported period and it's quite near or might be the same time of VS 2019 release.
4. Based on the problem mentioned above, VS 2017 has no "built in" tooling when creating new ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 project, but the external tooling may help. Therefore for those that still need to maintain ASP.NET Core 1.0/1.1 projects, we could use VS 2017 as long as the VS 2017 is still in support lifecycle or in mainstream support lifecycle.

I welcome feedback 🙂
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed issue "Feedback: ensure that .NET Core 1.0/1.1 tooling only supported in VS 2017 and won't be supported in VS 2019" (#1078)
